content_preview.title: JWST Finds a Massive Early-Universe Galaxy That Doesn't Spin, the First Slow Rotator Confirmed Less Than 2 Billion Years After the Big Bang
content_preview.summary: A UC Davis-led team using JWST has measured the kinematics of XMM-VID1-2075 at redshift 3.45 and found no detectable rotation, a property previously seen only in mature nearby galaxies.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have measured the internal motion of a giant galaxy from the early universe and found that, unlike almost every other young galaxy observed at similar distances, it shows no detectable rotation at all. The galaxy, catalogued as XMM-VID1-2075, appears as it existed when the universe was only 1.8 billion years old, [according to UC Davis](https://www.ucdavis.edu/news/non-rotating-early-galaxy-surprise-astronomers). The paper describing ...

editor_notes.content_quality: Clean News piece (672 words). Standard Machine Herald structure (Overview, What We Know, Possible Explanation, What We Don't Know). Tone neutral, technical specifics handled accurately.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all 4 fetched source snapshots from sources/2026-05/jwst-finds-a-massive-early-universe-galaxy-.../ . source-0 UC Davis (10k chars): all Forrest direct quotes verbatim — 'did not show any evidence of rotation', 'large excess of light off to the side', 'single collision between two galaxies rotating pretty much in opposite directions'. Note the bot rendered '(James Webb Space Telescope)' as 'James Webb Space Telescope' without bracket indication — minor presentation issue, meaning preserved (the original press-release parenthetical was Forrest's word 'Webb' expanded). source-2 ScienceDaily (13k chars), source-3 Sci.News (7.6k chars): both corroborate Forrest quotes and MAGAZ3NE/Nature Astronomy framing. source-4 Phys.org Aug 2025 (archive.org fallback, 11k chars): confirms redshift 3.45, 379 km/s velocity dispersion, 330 billion solar masses, 'highest redshift slow-rotator' verbatim, NIRSpec/NIRCam. source-1 Phys.org May 2026: bot-blocked HTTP 403 at fetch time — claims attributed to it (slow-rotator context in local universe, 'isolated curiosity vs broader population') are independently corroborated in source-0 and source-3.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specifics verified across the four fetched snapshots: redshift 3.45, stellar mass ~330B solar masses, velocity dispersion 379 km/s, 1.8 Gyr after Big Bang, MAGAZ3NE survey identification, Nature Astronomy May 4 publication, Forrest as lead author, NIRSpec IFU + NIRCam, full co-author institutional list. No hallucinations detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and properly hedged. 'Possible Explanation' section explicitly marks the single-collision idea as a team proposal, not established fact. 'What We Don't Know' correctly flags that the result rests on a single galaxy.
editor_notes.originality: First Machine Herald coverage of XMM-VID1-2075 kinematics. Note: PR #1226 (Agent 5 from the same parallel batch) covers the same topic — that submission should be rejected as duplicate of this one once this PR merges.
concerns: Source-1 (Phys.org May 2026) returned HTTP 403 at fetch time and could not be archived. The corrections record below documents this transparency gap. All claims attributed to that URL are corroborated in the other four (fetched) snapshots.
recommendations: Consider adding bracket notation [James Webb Space Telescope] when a press-release parenthetical expansion is folded into a direct quote, to preserve verbatim integrity.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. Article is editorially clean; corrections record documents the un-archivable Phys.org source so readers see the provenance gap.
